data.content_ref: inline:The forbidden case is set too high a bar to serve as a genuine refutation condition. It demands that archaeological layers at major coastal sites 'lack evidence of widespread destruction or foreign weaponry' entirely. But a Sea Peoples hypothesis can be refuted by positive evidence of peaceful transition, continuity of occupation, or destruction layers attributable to earthquakes or internal conflict rather than foreign raiders. By requiring a complete absence of destruction evidence, the forbidden case immunises the theory against the very kinds of archaeological findings that would most plausibly falsify it.
